_comment: Curated command reference dataset (F2). Hand-maintained source-of-truth for command reference cards. The indexer reads this, validates it, and emits 'command' entries into the unified index. Descriptions summarize behaviour documented in each tool's official man pages / docs; every card links back to that documentation (F6).

commands.title: nmap - Network Mapper
commands.summary: Scans hosts and networks to discover which are up, which ports are open, and what services and versions are running behind them.

flags.meaning: TCP SYN scan: sends a SYN and never completes the handshake, so it is fast and comparatively quiet. Requires elevated privileges.
Probe open ports to determine the service and version behind them.
Restrict the scan to specific ports, e.g. -p 22,80,443 or -p-  for all 65535.
Attempt OS detection from TCP/IP stack fingerprints.
Aggressive: enables OS detection, version detection, script scanning and traceroute at once.
Run the default set of Nmap Scripting Engine scripts.
Timing template from 0 (paranoid/slow) to 5 (insane/fast); higher is faster but noisier.
Write results in all three formats (normal, XML, grepable) at once.

examples.explanation: Scan every TCP port on one host and identify the service and version on each open port.

breakdown.role: detect the service/version on open ports
scan all 65535 TCP ports, not just the common ones

examples.explanation: Run default scripts and version detection across a whole /24 subnet and save results in all output formats.

breakdown.role: save normal/XML/grepable output to files named 'scan.*'
the target range: 256 addresses in CIDR notation

commands.title: dig - DNS lookup utility
commands.summary: Queries DNS servers directly and prints the answers, letting you inspect any record type and trace how a name resolves.

flags.meaning: Query a specific DNS server instead of the system default, e.g. @8.8.8.8.
Print only the answer, trimming all the diagnostic sections.
Trace the delegation path from the root servers down, showing each step of resolution.
Reverse lookup: find the hostname associated with an IP address.
Suppress every section then re-enable only the answer section for tidy output.
Record type to request; controls what kind of records are returned.

examples.explanation: Ask for the mail-exchange records of a domain and print just the answer.

breakdown.role: show only the answer, no diagnostics

examples.explanation: Resolve a name via Cloudflare's resolver and show the full delegation chain from the root.

breakdown.role: use this specific DNS server for the query
walk the resolution path from the root servers down

commands.title: netstat - Network statistics
commands.summary: Shows active network connections, listening ports, routing tables and per-protocol statistics on the local machine.

flags.meaning: Show TCP connections.
Show UDP connections.
Show only sockets that are listening (waiting for inbound connections).
Show numeric addresses and ports instead of resolving names (faster, avoids DNS).
Show the process id and program name that owns each socket (needs privileges).
Display the kernel routing table.
Show all sockets, both listening and established.

examples.explanation: List every listening TCP and UDP port together with the process behind each one - the classic 'what is running on this box' check.

breakdown.role: show the owning process for each socket

commands.title: ss - Socket statistics
commands.summary: The modern, faster replacement for netstat: dumps socket statistics and shows detailed connection state on Linux.

flags.meaning: Display TCP sockets.
Display UDP sockets.
Display listening sockets only.
Do not resolve service names; show raw port numbers.
Show the process using each socket.
Show both listening and non-listening sockets.

examples.explanation: Quickly list listening TCP ports and their owning processes - the ss equivalent of the common netstat check.

commands.title: nslookup - Name server lookup
commands.summary: Interactive and one-shot DNS query tool available on both Windows and Unix; handy for quick forward and reverse lookups.

flags.meaning: Set the record type to query, e.g. -type=MX or -type=NS.
Print the full response including headers for troubleshooting.
A trailing argument naming which DNS server to ask.

examples.explanation: Look up a domain's mail servers using Google's public DNS resolver.

breakdown.role: the DNS server to send the query to

commands.title: host - DNS lookup utility
commands.summary: A simple utility for performing DNS lookups; good for fast forward, reverse and record-type queries with minimal output.

flags.meaning: Specify the record type to look up, e.g. -t NS.
Equivalent to a verbose ANY query showing all record types.
Attempt a full zone transfer (list all records) - only works if the server allows it.

examples.explanation: List the authoritative name servers for a domain.

commands.title: whois - Domain registration lookup
commands.summary: Queries registry databases for registration details of a domain or IP block, such as owner, registrar and name servers.

flags.meaning: Query a specific whois server instead of the default for the TLD.
Hide the legal disclaimer / boilerplate text in the output.

examples.explanation: Retrieve registration and administrative details for a domain from its registry.

breakdown.role: the domain (or IP) to look up

commands.title: ping - Test host reachability
commands.summary: Sends ICMP echo requests to check whether a host is reachable and measures round-trip latency.

flags.meaning: Stop after sending this many packets (Linux/macOS).
Number of echo requests to send (Windows).
Interval in seconds between packets.
Ping continuously until stopped (Windows).

examples.explanation: Send four echo requests to a host and report whether they came back and how long they took.

breakdown.role: send exactly four packets then stop

commands.title: traceroute - Trace the network path
commands.summary: Maps the route packets take to a destination, listing each router (hop) along the way and the latency to it.

flags.meaning: Show hop IP addresses numerically without reverse DNS.
Set the maximum number of hops (TTL) to probe.
Use TCP SYN probes instead of UDP (helps past some firewalls).

examples.explanation: Show each router between you and the destination, numerically, to see where the path goes or stalls.

breakdown.role: print hop IPs numerically, no DNS lookups

commands.title: curl - Transfer data over URLs
commands.summary: A versatile command-line HTTP(S) client for fetching URLs, sending requests with custom methods and headers, and inspecting responses.

flags.meaning: Fetch only the response headers (HEAD request).
Include the response headers along with the body.
Follow redirects to the final destination.
Set the HTTP method, e.g. -X POST.
Add a custom request header, e.g. -H 'Authorization: Bearer ...'.
Send a request body (implies POST unless -X overrides).
Write the response body to a file.
Silent: suppress the progress meter and errors.

examples.explanation: Fetch just the headers and follow any redirects to see where a URL ultimately lands and what server responds.

breakdown.role: follow redirects to the final URL

commands.title: wget - Non-interactive downloader
commands.summary: Retrieves files over HTTP, HTTPS and FTP from the command line, with support for recursive downloads and resuming.

flags.meaning: Save the download under a specific filename.
Continue a partially downloaded file.
Download recursively, following links.
Quiet mode, no output.
Throttle the download speed to be polite to the server.

examples.explanation: Download a file and, if the connection drops, resume from where it stopped instead of starting over.

breakdown.role: resume a partial download if one exists

commands.title: nc (netcat) - TCP/UDP swiss-army knife
commands.summary: Reads and writes data across raw TCP or UDP connections; used to test open ports, transfer data, and build simple client/server links.

flags.meaning: Listen for an incoming connection rather than initiating one.
Verbose output, useful for seeing connection status.
Skip DNS resolution; use numeric addresses only.
Specify the local source port.
Zero-I/O mode: just check whether a port is open without sending data.
Use UDP instead of TCP.

examples.explanation: Check which ports in a small range are open on a host without sending any real payload - a quick connectivity probe.

breakdown.role: scan for listening ports without sending data
report the result of each connection attempt
the range of ports to test

commands.title: ssh - Secure Shell client
commands.summary: Opens an encrypted remote login/command session to another machine, and can tunnel other traffic securely.

flags.meaning: Connect to a non-default SSH port.
Authenticate with a specific private key file.
Forward a local port to a remote destination (local port forwarding).
Open a dynamic SOCKS proxy over the SSH connection.
Verbose; add more -v for deeper debugging of the handshake.

examples.explanation: Log in to a remote host on a custom port using a specific private key file.

breakdown.role: use this private key to authenticate
connect to port 2222 instead of the default 22
the account and host to log in to

commands.title: tcpdump - Packet capture and analysis
commands.summary: Captures and displays packets on a network interface using filter expressions; the standard command-line tool for inspecting traffic.

flags.meaning: Capture on a specific interface (e.g. eth0), or 'any' for all.
Do not resolve hostnames or port names; keep output numeric.
Write raw packets to a .pcap file for later analysis.
Read and display packets from a saved .pcap file.
Stop after capturing this many packets.
Print packet payloads in ASCII, handy for plaintext protocols.

examples.explanation: Capture DNS traffic on an interface and save it to a file for later analysis in a tool like Wireshark.

breakdown.role: save captured packets to a file

commands.title: gobuster - Directory/DNS/vhost brute-forcer
commands.summary: Discovers hidden paths, subdomains or virtual hosts on a target by trying entries from a wordlist and reporting which ones exist.

flags.meaning: Mode: brute-force directories and files under a URL.
Mode: brute-force subdomains of a domain.
Target URL (dir mode).
Path to the wordlist of names to try.
Append file extensions to each attempt, e.g. -x php,txt.
Number of concurrent threads.

examples.explanation: Look for hidden pages and files on a site by testing each wordlist entry, also trying common extensions.

breakdown.role: the list of names to try
also try each name with these extensions
